lessons.title: Generics and Collections: Applied Problems
lessons.summary: Practice tricky problems involving wildcards, type inference, and edge cases in collections.

contents.title: Key Points in Applied Generics
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Generics]] [[wildcard]] bounds problems
- Complex [[collection]] operation problems
- Applied sorting with [[Comparable]]/[[Comparator]]

These are applied exercises in the generics and collections area, which are frequently tested on the Gold exam.

contents.title: Pitfalls of Wildcards
contents.body:
You cannot add elements other than null to List<? extends Number>.
You can add Integer or its subtypes to List<? super Integer>, but when retrieving, the type is Object.
Accurately understanding these constraints is the key to passing the exam.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Type Inference and the Diamond Operator
contents.body:
The [[diamond operator]] from Java 7 onward infers types from the left-hand side.
In generic [[method]] [[type inference]], both the argument types and the return type are considered.
If type inference cannot be resolved, it may fall back to the Object type.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Edge Cases in Collections
contents.body:
Immutable collections created with List.of() and Map.of() cannot contain null.
[[Collections]].unmodifiableList() creates a list where changes to the original list are reflected.
List.copyOf() creates an independent copy that is not affected by changes to the original list.
Elements added to a [[TreeSet]] must implement Comparable or have a Comparator provided.
Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size list, but elements can still be modified.

contents.title: Common Exam Traps
contents.body:
List<Object> is not a supertype of List<String> (generics are invariant).
Calling add() on a list from Arrays.asList() throws an UnsupportedOperationException.
Calling set() on a list from List.of() also throws an UnsupportedOperationException.
Note that these [[exceptions]] are runtime exceptions, so [[compilation]] succeeds.
contents.note: Many exam questions test whether the code compiles versus whether an exception is thrown at runtime.

quizzes.question: Choose the result of executing the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Collections.unmodifiableList() returns a view of the original list. When an element is added to the original list with original.add(4), the change is reflected in unmod. Therefore, the size is 4.

quizzes.question: The following code has a compilation error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: List<? extends Number> represents "a list of some type that extends Number," but since the specific type is unknown, you cannot add elements other than null. numbers.add(10) causes a compilation error.

choices.text: You cannot add elements other than null to List<? extends Number>
ArrayList<Integer> cannot be assigned to List<? extends Number>
The return type of get(0) is Number and cannot be passed to println
new ArrayList<>() cannot be used with a wildcard type

quizzes.question: Choose the result of executing the following code.
quizzes.explanation: With var list = new ArrayList<>(), there is no type inference target for the diamond operator, so it becomes ArrayList<Object>. Although the String "hello" is stored as Object type, getClass() returns the actual runtime type, so "String" is output.

lessons.title: Stream API: Applied Problems
lessons.summary: Practice complex stream pipelines, Collector edge cases, and Optional chaining.

contents.title: Key Points in Applied Stream API
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Tracing complex Stream pipelines
- Applied usage of [[Collectors]] grouping and partitioning
- [[Optional]] chaining operations

The [[Stream API]] area is one of the most important topics on the Gold exam.

contents.title: Applied Collectors
contents.body:
Combining downstream collectors with [[groupingBy]] enables complex aggregations.
[[partitioningBy]] splits data into two groups based on a [[Predicate]] condition (true/false).
Collectors.toMap() throws an IllegalStateException when key collisions occur.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: flatMap and Optional
contents.body:
flatMap() is used to flatten a stream of streams.
Optional.flatMap() is used to prevent nested Optionals.
The or() [[method]] of Optional is available since Java 9.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Lazy Evaluation and Short-Circuiting
contents.body:
[[Intermediate operations]] in streams are [[lazily evaluated]] (not executed until a [[terminal operation]] is called).
findFirst() and anyMatch() are short-circuiting operations that stop processing once the condition is met.
limit() is also a short-circuiting operation that stops upstream processing once the required number of elements is reached.
Using peek() lets you observe the execution timing of intermediate operations, which helps in understanding lazy evaluation.
Operations that modify stream state inside forEach() can be problematic as side effects.

contents.title: Exam Question Patterns
contents.body:
Note that the return type of reduce() differs depending on whether an identity value is provided.
reduce(identity, accumulator) with an identity returns type T.
reduce(accumulator) without an identity returns Optional<T>.
Collectors.joining() is a Collector specialized for string concatenation.
On the exam, you may also be asked that forEach() on a [[parallel stream]] does not guarantee order.
contents.note: Use forEachOrdered() to guarantee order on a parallel stream.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: peek is an intermediate operation, so it is executed when the terminal operation count() is called. After filter, the remaining elements are "bb", "ccc", and "dd" — 3 elements. peek outputs each, then count() outputs 3. Note: In Java 9+, count() optimization may skip peek, but this optimization does not apply when size-changing operations like filter() are included.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: filter(s -> s.length() > 10) fails for "hello" (length 5), resulting in Optional.empty(). map() on an empty Optional does nothing. orElse("default") returns "default".

quizzes.question: What happens when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: Collectors.toMap() throws an IllegalStateException when key collisions occur. "cat" and "car" both produce the key 'c', causing a collision. This can be resolved by specifying a merge function (third argument).

quizzes.question: Complete the sealed class definition.
quizzes.explanation: A sealed class uses permits to restrict which subclasses can extend it. It was officially introduced in Java 17.

lessons.title: Concurrency and I/O: Applied Problems
lessons.summary: Tackle applied problems on thread safety scenarios and NIO.2 path operations.

contents.title: Key Points in Applied Concurrency and I/O
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Thread]] synchronization and [[deadlock]] issues
- Understanding the behavior of [[ExecutorService]]/[[CompletableFuture]]
- NIO.2 file operations and Stream integration

Strengthen your overall skills with practical concurrency and I/O problems.

contents.title: Thread Safety Problem Patterns
contents.body:
Operating on non-thread-safe collections from multiple threads can cause ConcurrentModificationException or data inconsistencies.
Understand countermeasures using atomic variables and [[synchronized]] blocks.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Path Operations
contents.body:
Path.resolve() concatenates a relative path.
Path.relativize() computes the relative relationship between two paths.
Path.normalize() removes redundant elements (. and ..).
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: CyclicBarrier and ConcurrentHashMap
contents.body:
CyclicBarrier is a barrier that waits until a specified number of threads arrive.
Threads that call await() reach the barrier point, and all threads resume simultaneously once all have arrived.
[[ConcurrentHashMap]]'s compute() and merge() are executed as atomic operations.
ConcurrentHashMap also features the ability to specify a parallelismThreshold for parallel forEach() execution.

contents.title: I/O Exam Considerations
contents.body:
Files.lines() returns a Stream, so it must be closed with [[try-with-resources]].
Files.readAllLines() returns a List<String> and does not require stream closing.
Files.walk() does not follow symbolic links by default.
relativize() throws an IllegalArgumentException if both paths are not of the same type (absolute/relative).
contents.note: To follow symbolic links with Files.walk(), specify FileVisitOption.FOLLOW_LINKS.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The relative path from /home/user/docs to /home/user/photos/vacation is computed. Go one level up from docs (..), then navigate to photos/vacation. The result is "../photos/vacation".

quizzes.question: Which list allows elements to be safely added from multiple threads?
quizzes.explanation: CopyOnWriteArrayList is a thread-safe list implementation. It creates a copy of the internal array on writes, so reads are lock-free and fast. ArrayList and LinkedList are not thread-safe.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: normalize() resolves "." (current directory) and ".." (parent directory). /home/./user becomes /home/user, and /home/user/../user becomes /home/user, so the final result is /home/user/docs.

quizzes.question: Select all features that were officially introduced in Java 17.
quizzes.explanation: Sealed classes, pattern matching (instanceof), and text blocks are official in Java 17. Record classes were introduced in Java 16, and lambda expressions were introduced in Java 8.

lessons.title: Comprehensive Practice Challenge
lessons.summary: Take on comprehensive problems combining modules, annotations, exceptions, inheritance, and generics.

contents.title: Preparing for Comprehensive Problems
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to approach comprehensive problems that combine multiple topics
- Techniques for accurately tracing code execution results
- Time allocation strategies and review tips

This is a practice challenge simulating the actual Gold exam.

contents.title: Combining Exception Handling and Inheritance
contents.body:
There are constraints on [[checked exceptions]] when [[overriding]] [[methods]].
A sub[[class]] method can only throw the same or narrower [[checked]] [[exceptions]] than the superclass method.
This constraint does not apply to unchecked exceptions.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Combining Generics and Inheritance
contents.body:
When [[inheriting]] from generic classes, careful handling of type parameters is required.
There are cases of instantiating with a concrete type and cases of carrying over the type parameter.
[[Type erasure]] causes [[generics]] type information to be lost at runtime.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Annotations and Modules
contents.body:
Understand how to use @Deprecated and @SuppressWarnings("deprecation").
@Deprecated(since="9", forRemoval=true) uses attributes available from Java 9 onward.
[[Modules]] can specify transitive dependencies with the requires transitive declaration.
requires static represents compile-time-only dependencies that are not needed at runtime.
Questions combining these concepts may appear on the exam.

contents.title: Strategies for Comprehensive Problems
contents.body:
First, check for compilation errors. If the code does not compile, there is no need to consider the execution result.
Next, check for potential runtime exceptions.
Also verify catch clause ordering and [[multi-catch]] constraints (exceptions with a subclass relationship cannot be multi-caught).
Generic type compatibility requires careful examination.
Skip time-consuming questions and answer the ones you can solve with certainty first — this is the strategy for passing.
contents.note: Allow approximately 2 minutes per question on the exam. If you're unsure, mark it and move on.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Field access is resolved by the compile-time type (Base), so obj.name is "Base". Method calls, however, are resolved by the runtime type (Sub), so obj.getName() returns "Sub".

quizzes.question: The following code contains a compilation error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: An overriding method can only throw the same or a subclass of the checked exceptions declared by the superclass method. Since Exception is a superclass of IOException, declaring a broader exception causes a compilation error.

choices.text: The override throws a broader checked exception than the superclass
Animal.speak() does not actually throw an IOException
println cannot be used in Dog.speak()

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: In the inner try, RuntimeException("inner") is thrown and caught in the catch clause, which throws a new RuntimeException("caught"). The finally block always executes, printing "finally ", and then the outer catch clause prints e.getMessage(), which is "caught".
